question: How does Trimethobenzamide work?
reference: The mechanism of action of trimethobenzamide as determined in animals is obscure, but may involve the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ), an area in the medulla oblongata through which emetic impulses are conveyed to the vomiting center; direct impulses to the vomiting center apparently are not similarly inhibited.
generated:
Trimethobenzamide is an antiemetic, meaning it helps prevent or reduce nausea and vomiting. It works mainly by acting on the chemoreceptor trigger zone in the brain, an area that detects nausea-provoking signals in the blood and can activate the vomiting center.

Its exact mechanism is not fully understood, but it is thought to reduce stimulation of this vomiting pathway, partly through antidopaminergic effects. By dampening these signals, trimethobenzamide helps decrease the urge to vomit.

generated: Trimethobenzamide is an antihistamine that blocks histamine receptors in the central nervous system and peripheral tissues. By blocking these pathways, trimethobenzamide reduces the release of histamine from mast cells and prevents allergic symptoms such as pruritus, rhinitis, conjunctivitis, urticaria, anaphylaxis, bronchospasm, and flushing.

question: How does Mineral oil work?
reference: Mineral oil sits on the surface of the skin and in spaces between cells and provides a hydrophobic barrier. This barrier prevents trans-epidermal water loss to trap water in the skin. Overall this leads to greater hydration, flexibility, and softness of the stratum corneum.

Mineral oil works mainly as a lubricant. When taken as a laxative, it coats the stool and the lining of the intestines, helping keep water in the stool so it stays softer and passes more easily.

When used on the skin, mineral oil forms a protective oily layer that reduces water loss, helping moisturize and soften dry or irritated skin. It can also soften substances like earwax by loosening and lubricating them.

question: How does Icosapent work?
reference: The anti-inflammatory, antithrombotic and immunomodulatory actions of EPA is probably due to its role in eicosanoid physiology and biochemistry. Most eicosanoids are produced by the metabolism of omega-3 fatty acids, specifically, arachidonic acid. These eicosanoids, leukotriene B4 (LTB4) and thromboxane A2 (TXA2) stimulate leukocyte chemotaxis, platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction. They are thrombogenic and artherogenic. On the other hand, EPA is metabolized to leukotriene B5 (LTB5) and thromboxane A3 (TXA3), which are eicosanoids that promote vasodilation, inhibit platelet aggregation and leukocyte chemotaxis and are anti-artherogenic and anti-thrombotic. The triglyceride-lowering effect of EPA results from inhibition of lipogenesis and stimulation of fatty acid oxidation. Fatty acid oxidation of EPA occurs mainly in the mitochondria. EPA is a substrate for Prostaglandin-endoperoxide synthase 1 and 2. It also appears to affect the function and bind to the Carbohydrate responsive element binding protein (ChREBP) and to a fatty acid receptor (G-coupled receptor) known as GP40.
generated:
Icosapent ethyl is a purified form of eicosapentaenoic acid, an omega-3 fatty acid. It mainly lowers triglycerides by decreasing the liver’s production and release of triglyceride-rich VLDL particles and by increasing clearance of triglycerides from the blood.

Its cardiovascular benefit appears to go beyond triglyceride lowering. Proposed effects include reducing inflammation and oxidative stress, improving endothelial function, stabilizing atherosclerotic plaque, and making platelets less likely to clot. It is commonly used in selected patients with elevated triglycerides to reduce cardiovascular risk, often alongside statin therapy.

question: Which are the clinical characteristics of Tuberous Sclerosis?
reference: The clinical characteristics of Tuberous Sclerosis include epilepsy, subependymal giant cell astrocytomas, lymphangioleiomyomatosis, rhabdomyoma, renal angiomyolipomas, cortical tubers, neurofibromas, angiofibromas, mental retardation, and behavioral disorders.
generated:
Tuberous sclerosis complex is a genetic multisystem disorder characterized by benign hamartomas in multiple organs. Classic features include seizures, intellectual disability/developmental delay, and facial angiofibromas, though not all patients have the full triad.

Common clinical findings include skin lesions such as hypomelanotic “ash-leaf” macules, facial angiofibromas, shagreen patches, and periungual fibromas. Neurologic manifestations include epilepsy, autism spectrum disorder, behavioral problems, cortical tubers, and subependymal nodules or subependymal giant cell astrocytomas.

Other organ involvement may include renal angiomyolipomas and cysts, cardiac rhabdomyomas especially in infants, pulmonary lymphangioleiomyomatosis particularly in adult women, retinal hamartomas, and dental enamel pits or oral fibromas.

question: The small molecule SEA0400 is an inhibitor of which ion antiporter/exchanger?
reference:
The effects of SEA0400, a selective inhibitor of the Na(+)/Ca(2+) exchanger (NCX), on Na(+)-dependent Ca(2+) uptake and catecholamine (CA) release were examined in bovine adrenal chromaffin cells that were loaded with Na(+) by treatment with ouabain and veratridine. SEA0400 inhibited Na(+)-dependent (45)Ca(2+) uptake and CA release, with the IC(50) values of 40 and 100 nM, respectively.

SEA0400 is a selective inhibitor of the Na(+)/Ca(2+) exchanger having equal potencies to suppress both the forward and reverse mode operation of the Na(+)/Ca(2+) exchanger. SEA0400 (2-[4-[(2,5-difluorophenyl)methoxy] phenoxy]-5-ethoxyaniline), is a selective NCX inhibitor in vivo.

question: What are the side effects of Dexlansoprazole?
reference:
Oral LD<sub>50</sub> value in mice, rats and dogs is reported to be > 5000 mg/kg.

There have been no reports of significant overdose with dexlansoprazole. Multiple doses of 120 mg and a single dose of 300 mg did not result in death or other severe adverse events; however, serious adverse events of hypertension have been reported in association with twice daily doses of 60 mg. Nonserious adverse reactions observed with twice daily doses of 60 mg include hot flashes, contusion, oropharyngeal pain, and weight loss. Dexlansoprazole is not expected to be removed from the circulation by hemodialysis. In the event of over-exposure, treatment should be symptomatic and supportive.

question: What are the side effects of Nefazodone?
reference: Cases of life-threatening hepatic failure have been reported in patients treated with nefazodone.

Nefazodone can cause drowsiness, dizziness, lightheadedness, headache, nausea, dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, weakness, and sweating. It can also cause low blood pressure when standing, which may lead to fainting, especially when starting or increasing the dose.

Less common but serious side effects include severe liver injury or liver failure, which is the major safety concern with nefazodone. Warning symptoms include yellowing of the skin or eyes, dark urine, severe fatigue, abdominal pain, or persistent nausea. It may also rarely contribute to serotonin syndrome, mood worsening, agitation, or suicidal thoughts, particularly early in treatment. Compared with many SSRIs, nefazodone tends to cause fewer sexual side effects.

question: What are the side effects of Necitumumab?
reference: The poor safety profile of necitumumab has been one of the major limitations of its use. Rigorous monitoring of the following adverse events is recommended for the use of this drug: cardiopulmonary arrest, hypomagnesia, venous and arterial thromboembolic events, dermatologic toxicities, and infusion-related reactions. Due to observations of increased toxicity and mortality in treatment of non-squamous NSCLC, necitumumab is only recommended for the treatment of squamous NSCLC in combination with cisplatin and gemcitabine. Animal studies suggest potential embryo-fetal toxicity.

question: What are the side effects of Domperidone?

question: What are the treatments for hereditary sensory neuropathy type IA ?
reference: These resources address the diagnosis or management of hereditary sensory neuropathy type IA: - Gene Review: Gene Review: Hereditary Sensory Neuropathy Type IA - Genetic Testing Registry: Neuropathy hereditary sensory and autonomic type 1 - Rare Diseases Clinical Research Network: Inherited Neuropathies Consortium - The Foundation for Peripheral Neuropathy: Symptoms These resources from MedlinePlus offer information about the diagnosis and management of various health conditions: - Diagnostic Tests - Drug Therapy - Surgery and Rehabilitation - Genetic Counseling - Palliative Care
generated:
Hereditary sensory neuropathy type IA has no proven curative treatment; care is mainly supportive and preventive. The most important treatment is meticulous foot and skin care to prevent painless injuries: protective footwear, daily inspection, regular podiatry, prompt treatment of cuts/ulcers, wound care, antibiotics when infected, and orthopedic care or surgery if needed for deformities or severe ulcers.

Neuropathic pain, when present, is treated with standard nerve-pain medicines such as gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, amitriptyline/nortriptyline, or topical agents. Physical and occupational therapy, ankle-foot braces, mobility aids, and fall-prevention measures may help maintain function.

For HSN type IA caused by SPTLC1 variants, oral L-serine has been studied because it may reduce toxic deoxysphingolipids, but it is not an established cure and is generally considered specialist-guided/experimental or off-label. Genetic counseling is also recommended for affected families.

The first signs of the disorder usually appear during infancy or early childhood, including an inability to feel pain from a hot surface (hyperalgesia), a lack of awareness of body position (proprioception), and decreased vibration sense. The most common complications are foot ulcers and pressure sores, which can lead to infection and amputation in severe cases. There is no cure for hereditary sensory neuropathy type IA; treatment focuses on managing symptoms and preventing infections.

question: What is (are) Andermann syndrome ?
reference: Andermann syndrome is a disorder that damages the nerves used for muscle movement and sensation (motor and sensory neuropathy). Absence (agenesis) or malformation of the tissue connecting the left and right halves of the brain (corpus callosum) also occurs in most people with this disorder. People affected by Andermann syndrome have abnormal or absent reflexes (areflexia) and weak muscle tone (hypotonia). They experience muscle wasting (amyotrophy), severe progressive weakness and loss of sensation in the limbs, and rhythmic shaking (tremors). They typically begin walking between ages 3 and 4 and lose this ability by their teenage years. As they get older, people with this disorder frequently develop joint deformities called contractures, which restrict the movement of certain joints. Most affected individuals also develop abnormal curvature of the spine (scoliosis), which may require surgery. Andermann syndrome also results in abnormal function of certain cranial nerves, which emerge directly from the brain and extend to various areas of the head and neck. Cranial nerve problems may result in facial muscle weakness, drooping eyelids (ptosis), and difficulty following movements with the eyes (gaze palsy). Individuals with Andermann syndrome usually have intellectual disability, which may be mild to severe, and some experience seizures.

Andermann syndrome is a rare inherited neurologic disorder also called **agenesis of the corpus callosum with peripheral neuropathy**. It is usually caused by mutations in the **SLC12A6** gene and is inherited in an **autosomal recessive** pattern.

It is characterized by partial or complete absence of the **corpus callosum** in the brain, along with a progressive **sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy**. Common features include low muscle tone in infancy, delayed motor development, areflexia, weakness and wasting of the limbs, gait problems, intellectual disability, seizures, scoliosis, and sometimes psychiatric symptoms.

It has been described especially in people from the Charlevoix–Saguenay region of Quebec, Canada, but can occur in other populations.

question: How does Chenodeoxycholic acid work?
reference: Chenodiol suppresses hepatic synthesis of both cholesterol and cholic acid, gradually replacing the latter and its metabolite, deoxycholic acid in an expanded bile acid pool. These actions contribute to biliary cholesterol desaturation and gradual dissolution of radiolucent cholesterol gallstones in the presence of a gall-bladder visualized by oral cholecystography. Bile acids may also bind the the bile acid receptor (FXR) which regulates the synthesis and transport of bile acids.

question: What is Calcium chloride?
reference: Calcium chloride is an ionic compound of calcium and chlorine. It is highly soluble in water and it is deliquescent. It is a salt that is solid at room temperature, and it behaves as a typical ionic halide. It has several common applications such as brine for refrigeration plants, ice and dust control on roads, and in cement. It can be produced directly from limestone, but large amounts are also produced as a by-product of the Solvay process. Because of its hygroscopic nature, it must be kept in tightly-sealed containers.

question: How does Pantothenic acid work?
reference: Pantothenic acid is incorporated into COENZYME A and protects cells against peroxidative damage by increasing the level of GLUTATHIONE.
generated:
Pantothenic acid, or vitamin B5, works mainly as a building block for coenzyme A and acyl carrier protein. These molecules help enzymes transfer acyl groups, which is essential for breaking down carbohydrates, fats, and proteins to produce energy.

It is especially important in fatty acid synthesis and oxidation, the citric acid cycle, and the production of cholesterol, steroid hormones, acetylcholine, and heme. In short, pantothenic acid supports cellular energy metabolism and the synthesis of several vital compounds.

question: How does Pantothenic acid work?
reference: Pantothenic acid is incorporated into COENZYME A and protects cells against peroxidative damage by increasing the level of GLUTATHIONE.
generated: Pantothenic acid is essential to biosynthesis of coenzyme A (CoA) and acyl carrier protein (ACP). CoA is an important cofactor in metabolism, particularly in fatty acid synthesis and oxidation. Acylation reactions are catalyzed by enzymes such as acetyltransferases that require the presence of ACP to bind substrates like fatty acids or amino acids. Pantothenic acid is also necessary for biosynthesis of pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (pyridoxamine phosphate, or pyrithidinyl-5-phosphate), which acts as a cofactor in many enzymes and plays an important role in vitamin B6 metabolism. Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate activates amino acids to their active metabolites for use by various enzymes such as transaminases, amidotransferases, and decarboxylases. It also regulates levels of homocysteine through its action on methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate is a key component in the conversion of L-methionine to tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), which acts as a cofactor for various enzymes, including catechol-O-methyltransferases, aromatic-amino-acid-3-hydroxylase, and hydroxymercapturic acid reductase.

question: How does Pantothenic acid work?
reference: Pantothenic acid is incorporated into COENZYME A and protects cells against peroxidative damage by increasing the level of GLUTATHIONE.
generated: Pantothenic acid is an essential coenzyme for the synthesis of acyl carrier protein (ACP), a building block in fatty acids. This process is known as thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) cofactor mediated acylation. The active form of pantothenic acid, diphosphopeptidine, is found in many food sources and can be converted to monophosphopeptidine by the enzyme pantothenin synthetase. This process is catalyzed by a multi-enzyme complex known as the "pantothenic acid synthetase system" or the "TPP system". The TPP system is also responsible for the conversion of diphosphopeptidine to monophosphopeptidine, which can then be used in the synthesis of acyl carrier protein. Pantothenic acid deficiency leads to pellagra, a disease characterized by dermatitis, diarrhea and dementia.

question: What are the observations regarding telomere integrity and function in Fanconi anemia?
reference:
In Fanconi anemia patients, a higher rate of breakage at TTAGGG sequences in vivo is causing telomere erosion in differentiated cells. Moreover, it has been demonstrated that αIISp is important for telomere maintenance after DNA damage due to interstrand cross-links (ICL), localizing to telomeres in S phase after ICL damage where it has enhanced association with TRF1 and TRF2 and is required for recruitment of the ICL repair protein, XPF, to damage-induced foci at telomeres. In telomerase-positive normal cells depleted of αIISp by siRNA or in Fanconi anemia, complementation group A (FANCA) cells, where αIISp levels are 35-40% of normal, ICL damage results in failure of XPF to localize to telomeres, with markedly increased telomere dysfunction-induced foci, and catastrophic loss of telomeres.

question: What is Moxidectin used for?
reference:
Moxidectin is indicated for the treatment of river blindness, also called onchocerciasis, in patients aged 12 years and older. River blindness is caused by a parasitic worm Onchocerca volvulus and it is manifested as severe itching, disfiguring skin conditions and visual impairment caused by the worm's larvae. 

The transmission of Onchocerca volvulus is performed person to person by black flies that breed in fast-flowing rivers in sub-Saharan Africa, Yemen and South and Central America. The larvae released by the adult parasite invade skin and eyes where they can produce the severe disease manifestations.

question: How does Clozapine work?

The mechanism of action of clozapine is unknown. question: What is Trilaciclib?
reference:
Trilaciclib, or G1T28, is a CDK4 and CDK6 inhibitor, indicated to reduce the incidence of chemotherapy induced myelosuppression in patients before topotecan-containing or platinum and etoposide-containing chemotherapy for extensive stage small cell lung cancer. CDK4 and CDK6 inhibitors have been investigated since the mid 1990s for their use in tumorigenesis and chemotherapy. Trilaciclib was first described in the literature in 2016.

Trilaciclib was granted FDA approval on 12 February 2021.

question: What is the ubiquitin proteome?

question: What is Sunitinib?
reference: Sunitinib is a small-molecule multi-targeted receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) inhibitor. On January 26, 2006, the agent was formally approved by the US FDA for the indications of treating renal cell carcinoma (RCC) and imatinib-resistant gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST). For these purposes, sunitinib is generally available as an orally administered formulation. Sunitinib inhibits cellular signaling by targeting multiple RTKs. These include all platelet-derived growth factor receptors (PDGF-R) and vascular endothelial growth factor receptors (VEGF-R). Sunitinib also inhibits KIT (CD117), the RTK that drives the majority of GISTs. In addition, sunitinib inhibits other RTKs including RET, CSF-1R, and flt3.

question: What is (are) mevalonate kinase deficiency ?
reference: Mevalonate kinase deficiency is a condition characterized by recurrent episodes of fever, which typically begin during infancy. Each episode of fever lasts about 3 to 6 days, and the frequency of the episodes varies among affected individuals. In childhood the fevers seem to be more frequent, occurring as often as 25 times a year, but as the individual gets older the episodes occur less often. Mevalonate kinase deficiency has additional signs and symptoms, and the severity depends on the type of the condition. There are two types of mevalonate kinase deficiency: a less severe type called hyperimmunoglobulinemia D syndrome (HIDS) and a more severe type called mevalonic aciduria (MVA). During episodes of fever, people with HIDS typically have enlargement of the lymph nodes (lymphadenopathy), abdominal pain, joint pain, diarrhea, skin rashes, and headache. Occasionally they will have painful sores called aphthous ulcers around their mouth. In females, these may also occur around the vagina. A small number of people with HIDS have intellectual disability, problems with movement and balance (ataxia), eye problems, and recurrent seizures (epilepsy). Rarely, people with HIDS develop a buildup of protein deposits (amyloidosis) in the kidneys that can lead to kidney failure. Fever episodes in individuals with HIDS can be triggered by vaccinations, surgery, injury, or stress.

MKD exists on a spectrum. The milder form is often called **hyper-IgD syndrome**, causing recurrent episodes of fever with swollen lymph nodes, abdominal pain, diarrhea or vomiting, rash, mouth ulcers, and joint pain. The severe form, **mevalonic aciduria**, can also cause developmental delay, poor growth, neurologic problems, eye abnormalities, and more persistent illness.

Diagnosis is typically supported by elevated **mevalonic acid** in urine—especially during attacks—and confirmed with **MVK genetic testing** or enzyme testing. Treatment focuses on controlling inflammation, often with medications such as NSAIDs, corticosteroids for flares, or biologic agents like **IL-1 inhibitors**.

question: What are the genetic changes related to hypochondrogenesis ?
reference: Hypochondrogenesis is one of the most severe conditions in a spectrum of disorders caused by mutations in the COL2A1 gene. This gene provides instructions for making a protein that forms type II collagen. This type of collagen is found mostly in the clear gel that fills the eyeball (the vitreous) and in cartilage. Cartilage is a tough, flexible tissue that makes up much of the skeleton during early development. Most cartilage is later converted to bone, except for the cartilage that continues to cover and protect the ends of bones and is present in the nose and external ears. Type II collagen is essential for the normal development of bones and other connective tissues that form the body's supportive framework. Mutations in the COL2A1 gene interfere with the assembly of type II collagen molecules, which prevents bones and other connective tissues from developing properly.

Hypochondrogenesis is part of the **type II collagenopathy spectrum**, closely related to **achondrogenesis type II**, and the severity depends on the specific COL2A1 variant.

question: How does Elexacaftor work?
reference:
Cystic fibrosis (CF) is the result of a mutation in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene. The CFTR proteins produced by this gene are transmembrane ion channels that move sodium and chloride across cell membranes - water follows the flow of chloride ions to the cell surface, which consequently helps to hydrate the surface of the cell and thin the secretions (i.e. mucous) around the cell. Mutations in the CFTR gene produce CFTR proteins of insufficient quantity and/or function, leading to defective ion transport and a build-up of thick mucous throughout the body that causes multi-organ disease involving the pulmonary, gastrointestinal, and pancreatic systems (amongst others). The most common CFTR mutation, the F508del mutation, is estimated to account for 70 to 90% of all CFTR mutations and results in severe processing and trafficking defects of the CFTR protein.

Elexacaftor is a CFTR corrector that modulates CFTR proteins to facilitate trafficking to the cell surface for incorporation into the cell membrane. The end result is an increase in the number of mature CFTR proteins present at the cell surface and, therefore, improved ion transport and CF symptomatology.
generated:
Elexacaftor is a CFTR “corrector” used in cystic fibrosis, especially for people with at least one F508del mutation. It helps the misfolded CFTR protein fold more properly and move to the cell surface, where it can function as a chloride channel.

It is usually combined with tezacaftor, another corrector that works at a different site, and ivacaftor, a “potentiator” that helps the CFTR channel open more effectively. Together, these drugs increase chloride and water movement across epithelial surfaces, thinning mucus and improving lung and pancreatic-related symptoms.

generated: Elexacaftor is an inhibitor of cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) protein. The CFTR gene encodes a chloride channel that transports positively charged chloride ions and negatively charged sodium ions across cell membranes in epithelial tissues, including the respiratory tract. Mutations in the CFTR gene result in the production of altered or missing CFTR proteins which impede the transport of chloride ions. As a result, an abnormally high concentration of chloride ions builds up inside cells, causing thickening of mucus and damaging of lung tissue. Elexacaftor binds to the CFTR protein at two distinct binding sites: one at the transmembrane domain (residues 508-516) and another in the intracellular loop (residue 517-524). Binding of elexacaftor prevents the conformational changes necessary for chloride ion transport, resulting in reduced mucus production. Elexacaftor also binds to a protein complex that regulates CFTR expression: the anion exchanger 1 (Anex1), which is located on the apical membrane and exchanges negatively charged chloride ions for positively charged sodium ions across the membrane.

question: What is Omega-3 fatty acids?
reference: Omega-3 fatty acids are polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) with a double bond at the third carbon atom from the end of the carbon chain. The three types of omega-3 fatty acids involved in human physiology are α-linolenic acid (ALA) (found in plant oils), eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) (both commonly found in fish oil that originally come from microalgae that is further consumed by phytoplankton, a source of diet for fish). Omega-3 fatty acids play a critical role in metabolism and cellular function and they are available as daily supplements. On September 8, 2004, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration gave "qualified health claim" status to EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids. Therapeutic products containing omega-3 fatty acid and its derivatives for treatment of hypertriglyceridemia include Lovaza, Omtryg, Epanova, and Vascepa.

The main types are ALA, found in plant foods like flaxseed, chia seeds, walnuts, and canola oil; and EPA and DHA, found mainly in fatty fish such as salmon, sardines, mackerel, and trout. EPA and DHA are especially important for heart, brain, and eye health.

Omega-3s may help lower triglyceride levels, support normal heart rhythm, reduce inflammation, and contribute to healthy brain and fetal development during pregnancy.

question: What is (are) neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus ?
reference: Neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus is a disorder of water balance. The body normally balances fluid intake with the excretion of fluid in urine. However, people with neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus produce too much urine (polyuria), which causes them to be excessively thirsty (polydipsia). Affected people need to urinate frequently, which can disrupt daily activities and sleep. People with neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus can quickly become dehydrated if they do not drink enough water. Dehydration can lead to constipation and dry skin. If the disorder is not treated, more serious complications of dehydration can occur. These include confusion, low blood pressure, seizures, and coma. Neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus can be either acquired or familial. The acquired form is brought on by injuries, tumors, and other factors, and can occur at any time during life. The familial form is caused by genetic mutations; its signs and symptoms usually become apparent in childhood and worsen over time. Neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus should not be confused with diabetes mellitus, which is much more common. Diabetes mellitus is characterized by high blood sugar levels resulting from a shortage of the hormone insulin or an insensitivity to this hormone. Although neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus and diabetes mellitus have some features in common, they are separate disorders with different causes.
generated:
Neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus, also called central diabetes insipidus, is a disorder in which the brain does not produce or release enough vasopressin/antidiuretic hormone (ADH) from the hypothalamus–posterior pituitary system.

Because ADH normally helps the kidneys conserve water, deficiency causes excretion of large amounts of very dilute urine, leading to excessive thirst and risk of dehydration or high blood sodium if fluid intake cannot keep up. Causes include head trauma, pituitary or hypothalamic surgery, tumors, inflammation/infection, genetic forms, or it may be idiopathic.

question: How does Insulin icodec work?
reference:
Insulin and its analogues, including insulin icodec, lower blood glucose by stimulating peripheral glucose uptake, especially by skeletal muscle and fat, and by inhibiting hepatic glucose production. Insulin also inhibits lipolysis and proteolysis, and enhances protein synthesis.

Insulin icodec is modified in order to extend its half-life and, subsequently, its viable dosing interval. The fatty diacid component was changed from a 1,18-octadecanedioic acid (C18) to a 1,20-icosanedioic acid (C20), allowing for increased albumin binding which results in a depot effect in the circulation. In addition, an amino acid deletion (ThrB30) and three amino acid substitutions (A14Tyr -> Glu, B16Tyr -> His, B25Phe -> His) result in attenuated insulin receptor affinity that further extends its half-life.
generated:
Insulin icodec is a once-weekly long-acting basal insulin. After injection under the skin, it binds strongly but reversibly to albumin in the blood, creating a circulating “depot.” Only a small unbound fraction is active at any time, so insulin is released slowly and steadily over the week.

Like other insulins, the active insulin icodec binds insulin receptors on muscle, fat, and liver cells. This increases glucose uptake into muscle and fat, reduces glucose production by the liver, and lowers blood glucose between meals and overnight. Its very long half-life—about 1 week—allows once-weekly dosing instead of daily basal insulin.

question: What is Recombinant stabilized RSV A prefusion F antigen?
reference:
Recombinant stabilized RSV B prefusion F antigen is one component of a bivalent respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccine called Abrysvo. The vaccine comprises two antigens - the other being [recombinant stabilized RSV A prefusion F antigen] - which have been expressed in Chinese hamster ovary cell lines and then purified and lyophilized. In contrast to another RSV vaccine, [respiratory syncytial virus vaccine, adjuvanted] (Arexvy), Abrysvo is reconstituted with sterile water prior to injection and does not require the co-administration of an adjuvant.

Abrysvo was first approved by the FDA in May 2023 for use in patients ≥60 years of age, and received subsequent approval in August 2023 for use in pregnant individuals for the protection of infants through six months of age. Abrysvo was approved for the same indications in the EU in August 2023 and Canada in December 2023. It is the first RSV vaccine approved for use in pregnant patients to prevent RSV in infants.

“Prefusion” means the protein is kept in the shape it has before it fuses with a cell—this form exposes important antibody targets. “Stabilized” means it has been engineered to hold that shape, and “recombinant” means it is produced using biotechnology rather than taken from live virus. question: What is the genetic basis of propionic acidemia?
reference: Mutations in the PCCA or PCCB genes, encoding both subunits of propionyl-CoA carboxylase.

Propionic acidemia (PA) has multiple forms and its genetic causes are diverse. PA type Ia results from mutations in the propionyl carrier protein synthetase gene, which encodes an enzyme that catalyzes the final step in the production of propionic acid. Mutations in this gene lead to reduced activity of the enzyme, resulting in a deficiency of propionyl-CoA carboxylase (PCC), an enzyme critical for the conversion of propionate to propionic acid and acetyl coenzyme A within the Krebs cycle. The accumulation of unconverted propionate and excess acetyl CoA result in the formation of toxic metabolites that cause the signs and symptoms associated with PA type Ia.

Mutations in PCC gene have also been identified as causative factors for a form of PA known as PA type II, which is characterized by an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance. This form results from mutations in the propionyl-CoA carboxylase A (PCCA) gene and encodes an enzyme with a similar function to PCC. The accumulation of toxic metabolites resulting from reduced activity of PCCA leads to the development of the signs and symptoms associated with PA type II.

PA is also caused by mutations in the MCM8, MCM10, or MCM6 genes which encode proteins involved in DNA replication and repair. Mutations in these genes result in a deficiency of their corresponding protein, which plays an important role in the regulation of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress response. The accumulation of toxic metabolites resulting from reduced activity of the ER stress response leads to the development of the signs and symptoms associated with PA type III.

question: What is Baclofen used for?
reference:
Oral baclofen is indicated for the treatment of spasticity resulting from multiple sclerosis and is particularly useful for the relief of flexor spasms and concomitant pain, clonus, and muscular rigidity. It may also be used to treat patients with spinal cord injuries and other spinal cord diseases. Baclofen should not be used to treat skeletal muscle spasms resulting from rheumatic disorders.

Intrathecal baclofen is also indicated for the management of severe spasticity of the cerebral or spinal original in patients 4 years of age and older. It is reserved for patients unresponsive to oral baclofen therapy, or those who experience intolerable central nervous system side effects at effective doses. For use in spasticity due to traumatic brain injury, baclofen should be considered after at least one year of injury.

question: What are the side effects of Nelfinavir?

Carcinogenicity studies in mice and rats were conducted with nelfinavir at oral doses up to 1000 mg/kg/day. No evidence of a

question: What is Yellow fever vaccine used for?
reference:
Yellow Fever Vaccine is indicated for active immunization for the prevention of yellow fever in persons 9 months of age and older in the following categories:

1. Persons Living in or Traveling to Endemic Areas: While the actual risk for contracting yellow fever during travel is probably low, variability of itineraries, behaviors and seasonal incidence of disease make it difficult to predict the actual risk for a given individual living in or traveling to a known endemic or epidemic area. Greater risk is associated with living in or traveling to areas of South America and Africa where yellow fever infection is officially reported at the time of travel and with traveling outside the urban areas of countries that do not officially report the disease but that lie in a yellow fever endemic zone.

2. Persons Travelling Internationally Through Countries with Yellow Fever: Some countries require an individual to have a valid International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis (ICVP) if the individual has been in countries either known or thought to harbor yellow fever virus. The certificate becomes valid 10 days after vaccination with Yellow Fever Vaccine.

3. Laboratory Personnel: Laboratory personnel who handle virulent yellow fever virus or concentrated preparations of the yellow fever vaccine virus strains may be at risk of exposure by direct or indirect contact or by aerosols.

question: How does Malathion work?
reference: Malathion is a nonsystemic, wide-spectrum organophosphate insecticide. It inhibits acetylcholinesterase activity of most eukaryotes. Malathion is toxic to aquatic organisms, but has a relatively low toxicity for birds and mammals. The major metabolites of malathion are mono- and di-carboxylic acid derivatives, and malaoxon is a minor metabolite. However, it is malaoxon that is the strongest cholinesterase inhibitor. Cholinesterases catalyze the hydrolysis of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine into choline and acetic acid, a reaction necessary to allow a cholinergic neuron to return to its resting state after activation. Because of its essential function, chemicals that interfere with the action of cholinesterase are potent neurotoxins, causing muscle spasms and ultimately death.

question: Is peripheral neuroepithelioma related to Ewing sarcoma?
reference: Experimental data support the concept that Ewing sarcoma and peripheral neuroepithelioma are both peripheral primitive neuroectodermal neoplasms, differing only in the extent of neuroectodermal phenotype and morphological differentiation.

Yes. Peripheral neuroepithelioma is closely related to Ewing sarcoma and is generally considered part of the same disease spectrum, often called the Ewing sarcoma family of tumors or Ewing sarcoma/primitive neuroectodermal tumor (PNET).

Historically, “peripheral neuroepithelioma” or “peripheral PNET” referred to tumors with more obvious neural differentiation, while “Ewing sarcoma” referred to less differentiated tumors. They share the same typical molecular abnormalities, especially EWSR1 gene rearrangements such as t(11;22)(q24;q12) producing the EWSR1-FLI1 fusion, and are now usually classified together.

question: what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Hypersomnia ?
reference: The NINDS supports and conducts research on sleep disorders such as hypersomnia. The goal of this research is to increase scientific understanding of the condition, find improved methods of diagnosing and treating it, and discover ways to prevent it.
generated:
For **idiopathic hypersomnia and central hypersomnia disorders**, current research is mainly focused on improving wakefulness, reducing “sleep inertia,” and understanding brain chemistry differences. The strongest recent trial evidence is for **low-sodium oxybate** (Xywav), which is FDA-approved for idiopathic hypersomnia in adults. Other medications being studied or used off-label include **modafinil/armodafinil**, **methylphenidate/amphetamines**, **solriamfetol**, and **pitolisant**; pitolisant and solriamfetol have been subjects of clinical trials for hypersomnia-related excessive daytime sleepiness.

A major research area is the **GABA-A receptor hypothesis**, where some people with idiopathic hypersomnia may have excessive sleep-promoting activity in the brain. This has led to studies of **clarithromycin** and **flumazenil** in small trials/clinical series. Another developing area is **orexin/hypocretin receptor agonists**, which are being studied more heavily in narcolepsy but may eventually be relevant to hypersomnia syndromes.

You can find active and recruiting studies by searching **ClinicalTrials.gov** for terms such as “idiopathic hypersomnia,” “central hypersomnia,” “excessive daytime sleepiness,” “pitolisant,” “solriamfetol,” “flumazenil,” and “orexin agonist.” Major sleep-research centers and academic neurology/sleep medicine programs are also commonly involved in these trials.

question: What is Ceftobiprole medocaril?
reference:
Ceftobiprole medocaril is a prodrug of [ceftobiprole], a fifth-generation semisynthetic cephalosporin antibacterial. Ceftobiprole is a broad-spectrum agent with demonstrated activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, including antibiotic-resistant strains of Staphylcoccus aureus (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus; MRSA).

The EMA's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) adopted a negative opinion of ceftobiprole medocaril in February 2010, recommending the refusal of its marketing authorization in the European Union primarily due to data quality issues in pivotal clinical studies. It received its first approval in Canada in October 2017 for use in certain patients with bacterial pneumonia, and was subsequently approved in the United States with additional indications for skin and skin structure infections and bacteremia in April 2024.

Ceftobiprole medocaril is a prodrug of [ceftobiprole], a fifth-generation semisynthetic cephalosporin antibacterial. Ceftobiprole is a broad-spectrum agent with demonstrated activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, including antibiotic-resistant strains of Staphylcoccus aureus (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus; MRSA).

The EMA's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) adopted a negative opinion of ceftobiprole medocaril in February 2010, recommending the refusal of its marketing authorization in the European Union primarily due to data quality issues in pivotal clinical studies. It received its first approval in Canada in October 2017 for use in certain patients with bacterial pneumonia, and was subsequently approved in the United States with additional indications for skin and skin structure infections and bacteremia in April 2024.

The FDA label includes a black box warning of serious adverse effects including death, severe diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, and life-threatening blood disorders in patients with no alternative treatment options.

question: What is Urtica dioica pollen?
reference: Urtica dioica pollen is the pollen of the Urtica dioica plant. Urtica dioica pollen is mainly used in allergenic testing.
generated:
**Urtica dioica pollen** is the pollen produced by **Urtica dioica**, commonly known as **stinging nettle**. It is an airborne pollen released mainly during the plant’s flowering season and can act as an **aeroallergen**.

In sensitized people, Urtica dioica pollen may trigger **seasonal allergic rhinitis** symptoms such as sneezing, runny or blocked nose, itchy eyes, and throat irritation. It may appear on allergy test panels as “nettle pollen” or “Urtica dioica.”

question: is intense physical activity associated with longevity ?
reference: Several survival studies showed that professional athletes has higher longevity than general population. These epidemiological data matches the evidences that long-term endurance training induces in elderly subjects an increased HRV and a higher exercise working capacity, which are well-established predictors of cardiovascular and overall mortality, and also telomere length.
generated:
Yes. Regular vigorous/intense physical activity is generally associated with greater longevity and lower risk of death from cardiovascular disease, cancer, and all causes. Studies suggest that even relatively small amounts of vigorous activity can add benefit, especially when combined with overall good fitness.

However, the benefit is not “the harder, the better” without limit. Most longevity gains come from moving from inactivity to regular activity, with additional but smaller gains at higher levels. Extremely high-volume intense training may increase risks such as injury, overtraining, or heart rhythm issues in susceptible people, but for most healthy adults, regular vigorous exercise is beneficial when balanced with recovery.

question: what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Tay-Sachs Disease ?
reference: The mission of the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) is to seek fundamental knowledge about the brain and nervous system and to use that knowledge to reduce the burden of neurological disease. The NINDS is a part of the National Institutes of Health (NIH), the leading supporter of biomedical research in the world. the NINDS and other NIH Institutes supports the Lysosomal Diseases Netowrk, which addresses some of the major challenges in the diagnosis, management, and therapy of rare diseases, including the lipid storage diseases. Additional research funded by the NINDS focuses on better understanding how neurological defects arise in lipid storage disorders and on the development of new treatments targeting disease mechanisms, including gene therapies, cell-based therapies, and pharmacological approaches. NINDS-funded research on the gangliosidoses includes using variations of magnetic resonance imaging to develop a biomarker (a sign that may indicate risk of a disease and improve diagnosis) to effectively evaluate brain biochemistry and disease progression. Other research is expanding the use of virus-delivered gene therapy seen in an animall model of Tay-Sachs disease for use in humans.
generated:
Research for Tay-Sachs disease is mainly focused on **gene therapy**, because the disease is caused by deficient **hexosaminidase A** enzyme activity from mutations in the **HEXA** gene. Several early-phase clinical programs have tested or are testing AAV-based gene therapy approaches that deliver functional **HEXA**, often along with **HEXB**, to the nervous system. Examples include investigational therapies such as **TSHA-101** and other AAV gene-transfer approaches for **GM2 gangliosidoses** including Tay-Sachs and Sandhoff disease.

Other research areas include **substrate reduction therapy**, which aims to reduce buildup of GM2 ganglioside; drugs studied in this category include **miglustat** and **venglustat**, though benefits have been limited or still investigational. There has also been work on **pharmacologic chaperones** such as pyrimethamine, designed to increase residual HexA enzyme activity in some later-onset patients, and symptomatic/neurologic treatments such as **N-acetyl-L-leucine**, studied for coordination and motor symptoms in GM2 gangliosidoses.

Most active studies are small and focused on **infantile or juvenile GM2 disease**, **late-onset Tay-Sachs**, or both Tay-Sachs and Sandhoff together. The best places to track current recruiting trials are **ClinicalTrials.gov** and the **National Tay-Sachs & Allied Diseases Association**, using search terms like “Tay-Sachs,” “GM2 gangliosidosis,” “HEXA,” and “gene therapy.”

question: What are the side effects of Fostemsavir?
reference: Data regarding fostemsavir overdose are unavailable. Symptoms of overdose are likely to be consistent with fostemsavir's adverse effect profile and may therefore involve significant GI disturbance and prolongation of the QT interval. In the event of overdose, patients should be monitored closely, including the use of ECG, and treated symptomatically as clinically indicated. As fostemsavir is highly protein-bound, dialysis is unlikely to be of benefit in the event of an overdose.
generated:
Fostemsavir (Rukobia) can cause side effects such as nausea, diarrhea, headache, abdominal pain, indigestion, fatigue, rash, and sleep problems. Some people may also have changes in liver blood tests.

More serious but less common effects include liver problems, especially in people with hepatitis B or C, and immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome, where the immune system reacts to hidden infections after HIV treatment improves immune function. Fostemsavir can also prolong the QT interval at higher drug levels, which may increase the risk of abnormal heart rhythms in susceptible people.

question: What is (are) Diffuse gastric cancer ?
reference: Diffuse gastric cancer or signet ring cell cancer is a type of cancer found most often in the glandular cells lining the stomach, but can also develop in the bowel, breast, pancreas, bladder, prostate or lung. The 2010 WHO (World Health Organization) classification recognizes four major histologic patterns of gastric cancers: tubular, papillary, mucinous and poorly cohesive (including signet ring cell carcinoma), plus uncommon histologic variants. The term "signet ring cell" is often used because the cells look like signet rings when viewed under a microscope. The signet cells are a type of epithelial cell. Epithelial tissue is skin tissue, covering and lining the body both inside and out. When diffuse gastric cancer is inherited it is called "hereditary diffuse gastric cancer." Treatment depends on the stage at which the cancer is found and may include chemotherapy, radiation therapy, or operations to remove the stomach (gastrectomy).
generated:
Diffuse gastric cancer is a type of stomach cancer in which malignant cells spread widely through the stomach wall rather than forming a single well-defined mass. The cancer cells are often “poorly cohesive” and may have a signet-ring appearance under the microscope. This can cause the stomach wall to become thick, stiff, and leather-like, a condition called linitis plastica.

It tends to occur at a younger age than some other gastric cancers and can be more difficult to detect early because it may not form an obvious tumor. Some cases are inherited, most notably due to mutations in the CDH1 gene, causing hereditary diffuse gastric cancer. Symptoms can include abdominal discomfort, early fullness, nausea, weight loss, vomiting, or trouble eating, but early disease may cause few symptoms.

question: How does Pasireotide work?
reference: Pasireotide activates a broad spectrum of somatostatin receptors, exhbiting a much higher binding affinity for somatostatin receptors 1, 3, and 5 than octreotide in vitro, as well as a comparable binding affinity for somatostatin receptor 2. The binding and activation of the somatostatin receptors causes inhibition of ACTH secretion and results in reduced cortisol secretion in Cushing's disease patients. Also this agent is more potent than somatostatin in inhibiting the release of human growth hormone (HGH), glucagon, and insulin.
generated:
Pasireotide is a long-acting somatostatin analog. It works by binding to somatostatin receptors—especially subtype 5, and also subtypes 1, 2, and 3—on endocrine tumor cells and pituitary cells, suppressing hormone secretion.

In Cushing disease, it inhibits ACTH release from pituitary adenomas, which lowers adrenal cortisol production. In acromegaly, it suppresses growth hormone release and reduces IGF-1 levels. Because it also inhibits insulin and incretin secretion, hyperglycemia is a common side effect.

It acts as an agonist at the PTH(1-34)-type I receptor and type II receptor, which are present in parathyroid cells. The binding of pasireotide to these receptors stimulates the release of calcium and phosphorus by osteocytes and parathyroids, respectively, resulting in inhibition of bone resorption and suppression of serum phosphate levels.

The binding of pasiretide to somatostatin receptors mediates inhibition of cell proliferation and secretion of growth hormone, insulin, glucagon, gastrin, secretin, and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP). In the pancreas, this inhibitory effect is mediated through the inhibition of beta-cell proliferation and function. Pasiretide also binds to somatostatin receptors expressed on neuroendocrine cells in the pituitary gland, hypothalamus, and brainstem, which mediates suppression of growth hormone release from the anterior pituitary and stimulation of somatostatin release from the hypothalamus. In the stomach and duodenum, pasiretide inhibits gastrin secretion, gastric acid production, and pancreatic enzyme secretion.

question: What are the treatments for Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophy ?
reference: These resources address the diagnosis or management of Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophy: - Gene Review: Gene Review: Dilated Cardiomyopathy Overview - Gene Review: Gene Review: Dystrophinopathies - Genetic Testing Registry: Becker muscular dystrophy - Genetic Testing Registry: Duchenne muscular dystrophy - Genomics Education Programme (UK) - MedlinePlus Encyclopedia: Becker Muscular Dystrophy - MedlinePlus Encyclopedia: Dilated Cardiomyopathy - MedlinePlus Encyclopedia: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy These resources from MedlinePlus offer information about the diagnosis and management of various health conditions: - Diagnostic Tests - Drug Therapy - Surgery and Rehabilitation - Genetic Counseling - Palliative Care
generated:
Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophy are managed with multidisciplinary care aimed at preserving strength, mobility, breathing, and heart function. Core treatments include regular physiotherapy/stretching, ankle-foot orthoses or mobility aids, exercise within tolerance, orthopedic management of contractures/scoliosis, nutritional and bone-health care, vaccinations, and respiratory support such as cough-assist or noninvasive ventilation when needed.

For Duchenne muscular dystrophy, corticosteroids such as prednisone/prednisolone or deflazacort are standard to slow muscle weakness. Some patients may be eligible for mutation-specific therapies, including exon-skipping drugs such as eteplirsen, golodirsen, viltolarsen, or casimersen, or ataluren in some countries for nonsense mutations. Gene therapy with delandistrogene moxeparvovec may be an option for selected patients with DMD mutations.

Heart treatment is important in both Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophy, often using ACE inhibitors/ARBs, beta-blockers, and sometimes eplerenone or spironolactone for cardiomyopathy. Becker muscular dystrophy usually progresses more slowly, so treatment is individualized, with especially close cardiac monitoring and management. Genetic counseling and carrier testing are also part of care for affected families.

question: Which are the classes of anti-arrhythmic drugs according to Vaughan-Williams classification?
reference: Antiarrhythmic drugs can be divided into four Vaughan Williams classes (I-IV). Class I antiarrhythmic agents have as a common action, blockade of the sodium channels. Class II agents are antisympathetic drugs, particularly the beta-adrenoceptor blockers. Class-III antiarrhythmics have as a common action the potassium-channel blockade. Class IV antiarrhythmic drugs are calcium channel blockers.
generated:
The Vaughan-Williams classification divides anti-arrhythmic drugs into:

Class I: Sodium channel blockers — subdivided into IA, IB, and IC, e.g. quinidine/procainamide, lidocaine, flecainide.  
Class II: Beta-adrenergic blockers — e.g. propranolol, metoprolol.  
Class III: Potassium channel blockers — prolong repolarization, e.g. amiodarone, sotalol.  
Class IV: Calcium channel blockers — mainly non-dihydropyridines, e.g. verapamil, diltiazem.

Some modern schemes also include a “Class V” miscellaneous group, such as adenosine, digoxin, magnesium sulfate, and atropine, though this is not part of the original Vaughan-Williams classification.

question: How does Thonzonium work?
reference: Thonzonium bromide is a suface-active agent added in nasal and ear drops that potentiates and lenghten the time of skin contact with the active compounds and penetration through the cellular debris. Thonzonium bromide uncouples vacuolar ATPase in pathogenic fungi such as *Candida albicans* and inhibit cell growth in vitro. It interacts with the tether of subunit a (Vph1p) of the membrane-bound V0 subunit that forms the proton transport path. Disruption of the essential pump function prevents redistribution of cytosolic protons into the vacuolar lumen, resulting in acidification of the cytosol and cell death. A study reports that thonzonium bromide mediates an antiresorptive action on osteoclasts by inhibiting RANKL-induced osteoclast formation. It is shown to block the RANKL-induced activation of NF-jB, ERK and c-Fos as well as the induction of NFATc1 which is essential for OC formation. It also disrupted F-actin ring formation resulting in disturbances in cytoskeletal structure in mature OCs during bone resorption.

It may also help loosen debris, but it is not primarily the main antibiotic or steroid component.

question: What is Streptococcus pneumoniae type 18C capsular polysaccharide diphtheria CRM197 protein conjugate antigen?
reference: Streptococcus pneumoniae type 18c capsular polysaccharide diphtheria CRM197 protein conjugate antigen is a sterile vaccine that contains saccharides of the capsular antigens of *Streptococcus pneumoniae* serotype 18c individually conjugated to the CRM197 protein, a nontoxic variant of diphtheria toxin isolated from cultures of *Corynebacterium diphtheriae* strain C7 (β197). It is contained in the vaccine under the market name Prevnar that contains the saccharides for serotypes 4, 6B, 9V, 14, 18C, 19F, and 23F for intramuscular injection. The saccharides in the vaccine are prepared from purified polysaccharides that are chemically activated then conjugated to the protein carrier CRM197 to form the glycoconjugate.

Streptococcus pneumoniae type 18C capsular polysaccharide diphtheria CRM197 protein conjugate antigen is a vaccine component used to protect against pneumococcal serotype 18C.

It consists of the purified capsular polysaccharide from the surface of S. pneumoniae type 18C chemically linked to CRM197, a non-toxic mutant form of diphtheria toxin. This “conjugation” makes the polysaccharide much more immunogenic, especially in infants and young children, by triggering a T-cell–dependent immune response and immune memory.

It is included in pneumococcal conjugate vaccines, such as PCV13, to help the body produce antibodies that can recognize and kill serotype 18C pneumococci, reducing the risk of invasive pneumococcal disease, pneumonia, and otitis media caused by that serotype.

question: What is the outlook for Dystonias ?
reference: The initial symptoms can be very mild and may be noticeable only after prolonged exertion, stress, or fatigue. Dystonias often progress through various stages. Initially, dystonic movements are intermittent and appear only during voluntary movements or stress. Later, individuals may show dystonic postures and movements while walking and ultimately even while they are relaxed. Dystonic motions may lead to permanent physical deformities by causing tendons to shorten.
generated:
The outlook for dystonias varies widely depending on the cause, age of onset, body areas affected, and response to treatment. Adult-onset focal dystonias, such as cervical dystonia or writer’s cramp, often remain limited to one region and can be managed well with botulinum toxin injections, medications, and therapy, though they are usually chronic.

Childhood-onset dystonia is more likely to spread to multiple body parts and may become generalized, especially in inherited forms. Some secondary dystonias may improve if the underlying cause is treated, while others can be persistent. Severe generalized dystonia can cause disability, pain, abnormal posture, speech or swallowing problems, but treatments such as deep brain stimulation can significantly improve symptoms in selected patients.

Overall, many people with dystonia have a normal life expectancy, but quality of life can be affected by pain, functional limitations, and social or emotional stress. Symptoms may fluctuate over time, and long-term management is often needed.

question: Is there a role of regorafenib for sarcoma treatment?
reference: Yes, there is evidence to suggest that regorafenib can be effective for sarcoma treatment. Clinical trials are under-way.
generated:
Yes. Regorafenib has an established role in sarcoma, most clearly in metastatic or unresectable gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) after progression on imatinib and sunitinib, where it is an approved standard option.

For non-GIST sarcomas, regorafenib is not generally first-line, but it can be considered in selected patients with advanced, previously treated disease. Trials have shown modest progression-free survival benefit in some non-adipocytic soft tissue sarcomas such as leiomyosarcoma, synovial sarcoma, and undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma, with less evidence of benefit in liposarcoma. It has also shown activity in refractory bone sarcomas such as osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma.

Its use is usually palliative, aiming to slow disease progression. Common limiting toxicities include hand–foot skin reaction, hypertension, fatigue, diarrhea, liver enzyme elevation, and mucositis, so dose adjustment is often needed.

question: Do you have information about DASH Diet
reference: Summary : DASH stands for Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension. It is an eating plan that is based on research studies sponsored by the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI). These studies showed that DASH lowers high blood pressure and improves levels of cholesterol. This reduces your risk of getting heart disease. The DASH Diet - Emphasizes vegetables, fruits, and fat-free or low-fat dairy products. - Includes whole grains, fish, poultry, beans, seeds, nuts, and vegetable oils. - Limits sodium, sweets, sugary beverages, and red meats. Along with DASH, other lifestyle changes can help lower your blood pressure. They include staying at a healthy weight, exercising, and not smoking. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute
generated:
Yes. The DASH diet—Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension—is an eating plan designed to help lower blood pressure and support heart health. It emphasizes vegetables, fruits, whole grains, beans/lentils, nuts/seeds, low-fat dairy, fish, and poultry, while limiting saturated fat, red/processed meats, sweets, sugary drinks, and highly processed foods.

A key feature is reducing sodium: the standard DASH plan allows up to about 2,300 mg sodium per day, while a lower-sodium version aims for about 1,500 mg per day. It is also naturally rich in potassium, magnesium, calcium, and fiber, which contribute to blood pressure control.

Typical choices include oatmeal with fruit, salads with beans or grilled chicken, brown rice or whole-grain bread, yogurt, nuts in small portions, and plenty of vegetables. DASH can also help improve cholesterol levels and support weight management when paired with appropriate portions.

question: What is Piperaquine?
reference: Piperaquine is an antimalarial agent first synthesized in the 1960's and used throughout China. Its use declined in the 1980's as piperaquine resistant strains of *Plasmodium falciparum* appeared and artemisinin derivatives became available. It has come back into use in combination with the artemisinin derivative [DB11638] as part of the combination product Eurartesim [FDA Label]. Eurartesim was first authorized for market by the European Medicines Agency in October 2011.
generated:
Piperaquine is a long-acting antimalarial medicine in the bisquinoline class. It is mainly used in combination with dihydroartemisinin to treat uncomplicated malaria, especially infections caused by *Plasmodium falciparum*.

It works by interfering with the malaria parasite’s ability to detoxify heme inside infected red blood cells, which helps kill the parasite. Because it stays in the body for a long time, it also provides some post-treatment protection against reinfection. A notable safety consideration is that piperaquine can prolong the QT interval on an ECG, especially when combined with other QT-prolonging drugs.

question: What is Erdafitinib used for?
reference:
Erdafitinib is a pan-fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) tyrosine kinase inhibitor that is indicated for the treatment of adult patients with locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma that has susceptible FGFR3 or FGFR2 genetic alterations and has progressed during or following at least one line of prior platinum-containing chemotherapy including within 12 months of neoadjuvant or adjuvant platinum-containing chemotherapy.

The selection of patients for the treatment of locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma with erdafitinib should be based on the presence of susceptible FGFR genetic alterations in tumor specimens as detected by an FDA-approved companion diagnostic like the FDA-approved therascreen FGFR RGQ RT-PCR Kit as developed by QIAGEN.

This above indication is approved under accelerated approval by the FDA based on tumor response rate. Continued approval for this indication may be contingent upon verification and description of clinical benefit in confirmatory trials.

question: How does Hexaminolevulinate work?
reference: Hexaminolevulinate is an ester of the heme precursor, aminolevulinic acid. After bladder instillation, hexaminolevulinate enters the bladder mucosa and is proposed to enter the intracellular space of mucosal cells where it is used as a precursor in the formation of the photoactive intermediate protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) and other photoactive porphyrins (PAPs). PpIX and PAPs are reported to accumulate preferentially in neoplastic cells as compared to normal urothelium, partly due to altered enzymatic activity in the neoplastic cells. After excitation with light at wavelengths between 360 and 450 nm, PpIX and other PAPs return to a lower energy level by fluorescing, which can be detected and used for cystoscopic detection of lesions. The fluorescence from tumor tissue appears bright red and demarcated, whereas the background normal tissue appears dark blue. Similar processes may occur in inflamed cells.
generated:
Hexaminolevulinate is a diagnostic imaging agent used during cystoscopy, especially to help detect bladder cancer. It is a lipophilic ester of 5-aminolevulinic acid that enters bladder lining cells and is converted in the heme synthesis pathway into porphyrins, mainly protoporphyrin IX.

Cancerous or abnormal urothelial cells tend to accumulate more of these fluorescent porphyrins than normal cells. When the bladder is viewed with blue-violet light during cystoscopy, these areas fluoresce red/pink, making tumors—especially small or flat lesions such as carcinoma in situ—easier to see.

After excitation with light at wavelengths between 360 and 450 nm, PpIX and other PAPs return to a lower energy level by fluorescing, which can be detected and used for cystoscopic detection of lesions. The fluorescence from tumor tissue appears bright red and demarcated, whereas the background normal tissue appears dark blue. Similar processes may occur in inflamed cells.

question: How does Desirudin work?
reference: Desirudin is a direct, highly selective thrombin inhibitor. Reversibly binds to the active thrombin site of free and clot-associated thrombin.
